query: transmission of HIV from mother to child

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Issues of Concern -- Maternal-Infant Transmission
retrieved_docs.content: Without treatment, the risk of vertical transmission from mother to infant is approximately 20% to 30%.[HHS. Perinatal HIV Clinical Guidelines. 2023] The effectiveness of treatment in preventing transmission depends on how early treatment is initiated and the viral load during pregnancy. ART with viral suppression throughout pregnancy and childbirth and ART for the infant for 4 to 6 weeks after birth has been shown to decrease the overall rate of HIV transmission to 0.1% to 0.5%.[CDC HIV Basics - Transmission. 2022][HHS. Perinatal HIV Clinical Guidelines. 2023] The risk of vertical transmission was shown to be around 1% for infants born to mothers with a viral load of 50 to 399 copies/mL near delivery, while the risk of transmission was 0.09% for mothers who had a viral load of less than 50 copies/mL. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV in Pregnancy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: HIV disease in humans can be caused by infection with either HIV-1 or HIV-2. HIV-1 is more prevalent of the two, has higher infectivity, virulence, and greater spread through heterosexual sex. The transmission rate of HIV from mother to child (vertical transmission) is 20% to 25% for HIV-1 versus about 5% for HIV-2. [1] Vertical transmission of HIV is possible not only throughout pregnancy but also during childbirth and breastfeeding. Together this is termed as the perinatal transmission of HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Other Issues -- Pregnant People and their Infants
retrieved_docs.content: Breast milk can also transmit HIV to the child. Using infant formula or donor human milk instead of breast milk eliminates the risk. Individuals who achieve and maintain viral suppression may opt to breastfeed with the understanding that the risk of transmission to the child is less than 1% but not zero.[HHS. Perinatal HIV Clinical Guidelines. 2023]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-2 Infection -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although the natural history may differ, the transmission of HIV-2 occurs by the same routes as HIV-1, including perinatal, sexual contact, and bloodborne exposure. During the mechanisms of transmission overlap, HIV-2 is a markedly less efficient virus and, thus, significantly less infectious. Globally, this has resulted in disproportionately fewer cases of HIV-2 and a more limited spread in comparison to HIV-1. The comparatively lower efficiency of HIV-2 has been well illustrated in studies examining mother-to-child transmission from the pre-antiretroviral era. Remarkably, perinatal transmission among HIV-2 positive mothers has been notably low at 1% to 2%, whereas in mothers with HIV-1, transmission ranges from 15% to 30%. [31] [32] Perinatal cohort studies from West Africa have demonstrated this is likely driven by factors specific to HIV-2, including lower pretreatment viral loads among peripartum women and decreased viral shedding in the female genital tract during delivery. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal HIV -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: One of the greatest advancements in medicine has been the prevention of mother-to-child transmission (MTCT) of HIV type 1 (HIV-1). The rate of transmission of HIV to neonates has been reduced to less than 1% with the implementation of appropriate strategies and careful planning. The increase in comprehensive serologic screening and the treatment of HIV-infected pregnant women has resulted in the reduction of vertical transmission. There are evidence-based prevention modalities that can be utilized at different stages of pregnancy and postpartum to improve outcomes. Antiretroviral therapies (ART) can be prescribed during gestation, antepartum during vaginal or elective cesarean delivery, postnatally to the neonate, or when breastfeeding. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal HIV -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The burden of MTCT is a worldwide epidemic, with an estimated 160,000 infants infected annually with HIV as of 2018. The majority of mothers and neonates infected with HIV are located in sub-Saharan Africa. [13] Overall, the rate of perinatal transmission of HIV has decreased substantially over the past 20 years to less than 1% in the United States (US) and Europe. [14] [15] In the US, approximately more than 5000 pregnant women are HIV positive. [16] In the year 2013, nationwide in the US, there were only 69 infants born with HIV infection, leading to an estimated incidence of 1.8 out of 100,000 live births for perinatally-acquired HIV infection. [14] The US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) has set goals to eliminate perinatal HIV spread, which has caused a significant decline in MTCT transmission. The goal is to reduce the incidence of perinatal HIV to less than 1 in 100,000 births. [14] During the peak of HIV transmission in 1991, the reported incidence of neonates born with HIV was 42.8 per 100,000 births, with a substantial decline to 1.3 per 100,000 live-born infants in 2015. [17] Due to racial disparities in healthcare, the incidence of perinatal HIV is 5 times greater in Black versus White infants. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV in Pregnancy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The study of HIV during pregnancy holds great significance because many women are first diagnosed with HIV during pregnancy. Similarly, it is equally important in cases where one or both partners are HIV positive and wish to conceive. During the recent years, universal HIV prenatal testing, antiretroviral therapy (ART), scheduled cesarean delivery for HIV positive women with elevated viral loads, appropriate ART for infants and avoidance of breastfeeding have shown encouraging results, and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention now aims to eliminate HIV transmission from mother to child by reducing its incidence to <1 infection per 100,000 live births.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Other Issues -- Pregnant People and their Infants
retrieved_docs.content: The global reduction in HIV transmission from mother to child is a significant public health victory, with universal testing and the offering of treatment being the cornerstones of prevention. The WHO has the triple aim to eliminate maternal-child transmission of HIV, HBV, and syphilis.[WHO. Global Health Sector HIV Strategies. 2022] All individuals, irrespective of risk status, should be tested if trying to conceive and as early as possible in pregnancy. Those who are at higher risk should have repeat testing in the third trimester, including in areas with HIV incidence.[HHS. Perinatal HIV Clinical Guidelines. 2023] Some states and territories mandate repeat third-trimester testing. HIV transmission is further reduced if rapid HIV testing is performed at the time of delivery if the HIV status of a client is unknown or if they live in a high-incidence area. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV in Pregnancy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: According to one estimate, around 5,000 HIV positive women give birth in the United States every year. [2] Following current recommendations regarding HIV and pregnancy, both the United States and Europe have witnessed a steep decline in perinatal HIV transmission to 1% or less. [3] In the United States, black infants have 5 times increased incidence of perinatal HIV compared to white infants. [2] According to a report by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, only 44 HIV positive infants were born in the United States in the year 2016, with the incidence of perinatal HIV transmission being as low as 1.1 out of 100,000 live births. [4] Globally, with the increasing availability of ART to pregnant women, the number of new HIV cases among children has decreased by 47% since 2010 according to the Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS (UNAIDS).

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal HIV -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: HIV is a ribonucleic acid (RNA) viral pathogen with 2 subtypes: HIV-1 and HIV-2. HIV-1 is the most common type worldwide and is more transmissible and progresses faster than HIV-2. Presumably, this Retroviridae family originated from wild chimpanzees in Central Africa. [3] The virus is transmitted across mucous membranes via penetrative unprotected sexual intercourse or intravenous drug use, blood transfusions in developing countries, vertical transmission, or through breastfeeding. [4] [5] The risk of transmission via lactation is about 12% to 14%, with the risk increasing in high viral load states. [6] [7] Overall, the probability of vertical transmission is about 25% without the utilization of appropriate ART therapy during pregnancy. Several risk factors that increase the chance of this transmission were observed in clinical trials. The risk factors include elevated maternal plasma viral RNA concentrations, maternal breast milk viral load, acute maternal seroconversion, advanced maternal disease, and decreased cluster of differentiation (CD)4+ T-cell count of the mother. [8] [9] [10] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Community HIV Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: Developing cost-effective recommendations and education to screen for HIV is a critical component of prevention. For example, the United States Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) recommends that clinicians in the United States screen all pregnant women and at-risk persons aged 15 to 65, with particular emphasis on individuals at high risk of infection. [20] Maternal HIV screening has resulted in a significant decline in mother-to-child HIV transmission, preventing nearly 22,000 perinatal infections between 1994 and 2010. [21] Treatment as prevention recognizes that HIV is not sexually transmitted if the viral load is undetectable (defined as <200 copies of HIV-1 RNA/mL of plasma). [22] [23] Otherwise known as undetectable=utransmittable or U=U, evidence is increasing of the effectiveness for those who share drug injection equipment. Emphasis on U=U is a strong motivator for patient adherence to medication. However, with undetectable viral loads, HIV transmission occurs perinatally and through breast milk. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Prevention of HIV ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Deterrence and Patient Education -- Prevention of HIV Transmission
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with HIV are often highly motivated to prevent transmission to others, particularly when they have a partner who is not infected with HIV. Recent data in the United States  reveal that every 10% increase in viral suppression on a population level is associated with a 4% decline in the incidence of HIV in the subsequent year. Clinical education and emphasis on undetectable equals untransmittable are crucial elements of HIV treatment, benefiting both the patient's health and that of their partner or partners. People of child-bearing age may be concerned about the transmission of HIV during pregnancy and should be aware of the options for treatment and the benefits of planning.

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Epidemiology -- HIV Incidence and Modes of Transmission
retrieved_docs.content: The number of people acquiring HIV has decreased by 38% from 2010 to 2022, and the rates in children have decreased by 58%. The HIV acquisition rate in Africa has shown a remarkable 3-fold decline from 2010 to 2022, from 1.75 to 0.57 per 1000 population, with the most significant improvements in Eastern and Southern Africa.[WHO. HIV Data and Statistics. 2023] Heterosexual contact is the most common route of infection worldwide, corresponding to the large number of infections that occur via this route in Africa, where half of all new global infections occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Human T-Cell Lymphotropic Virus -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are three major routes of HTLV-1 transmission: vertical transmission from mother to child via breastmilk (the most common route), sexual intercourse, and blood transfusions. [14] HTLV-1 has been isolated in breastmilk, and breastfed children are four times more likely to be infected than bottle-fed infants from infected mothers. [15] A longer duration of breastfeeding increases the risk of transmission, and it has been proposed that maternal antibodies transferred during pregnancy protect babies for some months post-natally. The risk to bottle-fed children is significantly lower but not negated entirely: 0.6% at one year and 4.6% at four years. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV in Pregnancy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The management of HIV positive pregnant patient does not end with the delivery of the baby. ART regimens for both the mother and the child have to be decided before hospital discharge. Future contraception options have to be discussed. Counseling about breastfeeding needs to be done. The patient’s family support and home environment need to be assessed by a social worker. Only by working as an interprofessional team can we minimize the perinatal transmission of HIV. [54] [57] [Level V]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The World Health Organization (WHO) estimates more than 630,000 people died from HIV-related causes in 2022.[WHO. HIV Data and Statistics. 2023] Thirty-nine million people are living with HIV, of whom two-thirds are living in Africa, and 1.5 million are children aged 0 to 14. In addition, 1.3 million people acquire HIV each year, most commonly from heterosexual contact.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2665
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a significant route of transmission. Antiretroviral agents such as delavirdin‑mesylate, efavirenz, and etravirine have been identified as medications that may prevent HIV infection, highlighting their potential role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and broader infection‑control strategies. Understanding both the behavioral risk factor of shared needle use and the preventive capabilities of these drugs is essential for effective patient counseling, risk reduction, and public‑health interventions aimed at curbing the spread of HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2117
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy encompass a range of maternal conditions that can affect pregnancy outcomes. Among these, قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (mild hyperemesis gravidarum) is identified as a specific child condition within the broader category of other pregnancy complications. Additionally, the category is classified to include الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before birth), highlighting the impact of substance use on maternal and fetal health. The classification also covers الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), emphasizing the importance of infectious disease management in pregnant patients. Recognizing these interrelated conditions under the umbrella of other complications of pregnancy aids clinicians in comprehensive risk assessment, targeted prevention, and appropriate therapeutic interventions to improve maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1229
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a significant route of transmission. Antiretroviral agents such as delavirdin‑mesilát and etravirine have demonstrated the ability to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their potential role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and broader infection‑control strategies. Understanding the connection between shared needle use and HIV transmission, together with the preventive capabilities of delavirdin‑mesilát and etravirine, is essential for effective public‑health interventions and patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a broad category that includes several specific maternal health issues. It has the child condition قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (light vomiting of pregnancy) and is classified as encompassing الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery), الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), and خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل (thyroid dysfunction in the gestational period). These various complications highlight the diverse risks that can affect pregnant individuals and underscore the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and appropriate management to protect both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy encompass a range of maternal conditions, including قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (light vomiting during pregnancy) and فقر الدم في أثناء الحمل (anemia during pregnancy). This category is also classified as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before birth) and الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), indicating that these specific complications are considered part of the broader group of other pregnancy‑related health issues. Recognizing these interrelated conditions aids clinicians in comprehensive prenatal assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1214
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, highlighting a major route of transmission that requires targeted harm‑reduction strategies. Pharmacologic agents such as delavirdin‑mesilát, lamivudin, and zidovudin have each been identified as having preventive potential against HIV infection, underscoring the role of antiretroviral medications in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and infection control. Integrating needle‑sharing risk mitigation with the use of these preventive drugs is essential for reducing new HIV cases and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1431
retrieved_docs.content: Influenza, referred to in Arabic as الالتهاب الرئوي, is a viral respiratory infection that can lead to or be associated with pneumonia. The broader term الالتهاب الرئوي also includes specific forms such as التهاب رئوي وليدي, which denotes neonatal pneumonia, a distinct subtype of pneumonia occurring in newborn infants. Understanding that influenza may precipitate pneumonia and that neonatal pneumonia is a particular manifestation of pneumonia aids clinicians in identifying at‑risk patients, especially infants, and in applying appropriate preventive and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2086
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a significant route of transmission. The antiretroviral agents delavirdin‑mesilát and elvitegravir are both recognized for their potential to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis and broader strategies to reduce the risk associated with behaviors such as shared needle use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 576
retrieved_docs.content: Abacavir / lamivudine is a combination antiviral medication that is classified as a member of the group “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1878
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a major route of transmission. Antiretroviral agents such as delavirdine‑mesylate and efavirenz have the potential to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their role in prophylactic strategies. In contrast, methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in individuals with HIV infection, indicating that this immunosuppressive medication should be avoided in this patient population. Understanding these relationships aids clinicians in both preventing HIV transmission and selecting safe therapeutic options for patients living with the virus.

retrieved_docs.content: Antepartum Procedures is a medical category that includes, as its child, the subcategory Antepartum Procedures: Application, Insertion, Removal, indicating that the broader group of antepartum interventions is organized into specific actions of applying, inserting, and removing devices or treatments during the prenatal period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1211
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. In patients with HIV infection, the use of methotrexate sodium is contraindicated, reflecting the need to avoid this immunosuppressive agent in this population. Conversely, the antiretroviral drugs delavirdin and delavirdin‑mesylate are noted for their potential to prevent HIV infection, highlighting their role in prophylactic strategies. Understanding these relationships—risk behaviors, drug contraindications, and preventive therapies—is essential for effective patient management and infection control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 706
retrieved_docs.content: HIV is a chronic viral infection that can be managed with antiretroviral therapy, and tenofovir disoproxil is an antiretroviral prodrug that may be used to treat HIV infection. Tenofovir disoproxil works by inhibiting viral replication, thereby helping to suppress HIV viral load, preserve immune function, and reduce disease progression, making it a key component of comprehensive HIV patient care.
